original_abstract:
Large language models (LMs) that have undergone instruction tuning exhibit an impressive capacity for zero-shot generalization across diverse tasks. However, the efficacy of these models often hinges on the availability of human-annotated instruction data, which is frequently constrained by limited volume, lack of creative diversity, and high collection costs. To address these limitations, we present SELF-INSTRUCT, a framework designed to enhance the instruction-following capabilities of pretrained LMs by bootstrapping their own internal knowledge and generative power.

Our pipeline iteratively generates new instructions, corresponding input contexts, and target outputs directly from the language model. To ensure data quality, the framework pipelines these generations through automated filters to remove low-quality, repetitive, or invalid samples before using the refined synthetic dataset to finetune the original model. We applied this method to a vanilla GPT-3 model, producing a dataset of approximately 52K instructions. Empirical evaluations on the SUPER-NATURALINSTRUCTIONS benchmark show that our approach yields a 33% absolute improvement over the base model, achieving performance levels comparable to specialized models like InstructGPT001, which are trained on proprietary human data. 

Further assessment using a newly curated set of expert-written tasks for novel applications demonstrates that SELF-INSTRUCT significantly outperforms models trained on existing public instruction datasets, nearly bridging the performance gap with InstructGPT models. SELF-INSTRUCT offers an effective, virtually annotation-free path for aligning language models with human intent. We have released our large-scale synthetic dataset and code to support the development of more robust, general-purpose instruction-following models.
humanized_abstract:
While large language models (LMs) that were fine-tuned on an instruction learning objective have been shown to exhibit strong zero-shot multi-task learning abilities, the instruction data is typically limited in size, not diverse and very expensive to obtain. In this paper, we introduce SELF-INSTRUCT: a method that leverages the self-supervised abilities of a pretrained LMs, combined with its own language generation capabilities, in order to augment and improve its ability to follow human-written language instructions.

The training pipeline of the NeurIPS paper generates new instructions, input contexts, and outputs in a loop, using the language model as a black box. The generated samples are then pipelined through a series of automated filters that eliminate low quality, or duplicate samples that are likely not what the model intended to output, to filter out the noise and what a human would consider not to be valid. The resulting filtered synthetic dataset is then used to do a finetune of the original model. We have demonstrated the approach with a vanilla GPT-3 model, and generated a synthetic dataset of about 52K instructions. Results on the SUPER-NATURALINSTRUCTIONS benchmark are 33% absolute improvement over the base model. Our results are comparable to InstructGPT001 which is a model that is trained on human-human data that was not publically available.

We evaluate the model further on a new set of tasks we curated as expert-written instructions for few-shot learning on a variety of novel applications. We show that the model is far outperforming state of the art models that are pre-trained on large public instruction-following datasets, and that it approaches performance of InstructGPT models. SELF-INSTRUCT provides an efficient and close to annotation-free method to train language models that are on-pose with the intended goals of the users. Our full experiments along with our large scale synthetic dataset and code are available in the following repository: https://github.com/BenZhou616/SELF-INSTRUCT

document.output:

Our full experiments along with our large scale synthetic dataset and code are available in the following repository: https://github.com/BenZhou616/SELF-INSTRUCT
